title: Secret prevention and response

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Secret prevention and response; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Secrets must enter through approved secret storage, be redacted from output, receive least privilege and expiry, and be rotated immediately when exposed.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A leaked API key is revoked first, impact and logs are reviewed, a replacement is issued with narrower scope, and repository history is remediated separately. Your goal is to review a leak scenario and execute a safe containment, rotation and history-remediation plan in a sandbox. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Issues, pull requests and review; Debugging and quality automation; Configuration and environment; Log, show and graph. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Secrets must enter through approved secret storage, be redacted from output, receive least privilege and expiry, and be rotated immediately when exposed. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Deleting a file or commit does not remove copies from clones, logs, caches, artifacts, or provider history, and encoded text is not encryption. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Moving a committed key into .gitignore or deleting the latest file makes the old credential safe again.

includes: Keep secrets out of repositories, logs and artifacts and rotate exposed values rather than merely deleting a commit.
Review a leak scenario and execute a safe containment, rotation and history-remediation plan in a sandbox.
excludes: Deleting a file or commit does not remove copies from clones, logs, caches, artifacts, or provider history, and encoded text is not encryption.

outcomes: Review a leak scenario and execute a safe containment, rotation and history-remediation plan in a sandbox.

examples.title: Secret prevention and response: direct case
examples.situation: A leaked API key is revoked first, impact and logs are reviewed, a replacement is issued with narrower scope, and repository history is remediated separately.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A leaked API key is revoked first, impact and logs are reviewed, a replacement is issued with narrower scope, and repository history is remediated separately.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Assume exposed credentials are compromised, revoke and rotate them, investigate use and copies, then clean history and prevention controls without treating cleanup as rotation.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Assume exposed credentials are compromised, revoke and rotate them, investigate use and copies, then clean history and prevention controls without treating cleanup as rotation.

examples.title: Secret prevention and response: boundary case
examples.situation: CI exposes a short-lived token only to the deployment job and masks it without echoing commands or uploading its environment as an artifact.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: CI exposes a short-lived token only to the deployment job and masks it without echoing commands or uploading its environment as an artifact.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Deleting a file or commit does not remove copies from clones, logs, caches, artifacts, or provider history, and encoded text is not encryption.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Moving a committed key into .gitignore or deleting the latest file makes the old credential safe again.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Review a leak scenario and execute a safe containment, rotation and history-remediation plan in a sandbox.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Moving a committed key into .gitignore or deleting the latest file makes the old credential safe again.
misconceptions.correction: Assume exposed credentials are compromised, revoke and rotate them, investigate use and copies, then clean history and prevention controls without treating cleanup as rotation.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Moving a committed key into .gitignore or deleting the latest file makes the old credential safe again.

analogy.example: A secured checkpoint system can model Secret prevention and response: each revision is inspected, credentials are restricted, incidents revoke access, and recovery preserves known-good state.
usefulFor: Connecting pipeline evidence, secret response, reversible change, and collaborative policy
limitations: CI isolation, credential propagation, Git reachability, and distributed copies require technical controls beyond checkpoint paperwork.
Deleting a file or commit does not remove copies from clones, logs, caches, artifacts, or provider history, and encoded text is not encryption.

recap.summary: You should now be able to review a leak scenario and execute a safe containment, rotation and history-remediation plan in a sandbox. Keep this boundary in view: Deleting a file or commit does not remove copies from clones, logs, caches, artifacts, or provider history, and encoded text is not encryption.

recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Restore, revert, reset and reflog”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
